segments.s274-g1: سپهدار ترکان is a possessive/compound phrase meaning “the commander of the Turks” (poetic Persian often omits the ezafe). برآراست is a 3rd-person singular perfective/past form meaning “he arranged/put in order” and follows the subject; کار is the object/accusative noun “affair/plan.”
segments.s274-g2: ز لشکر گزید is a prepositional origin phrase + past verb: ز (= from) لشکر (= the host/army) is the source, گزید is 3rd-person singular past “he chose.” آن زمان is a temporal phrase “at that time/moment,” and ده سوار is a numeral + noun “ten horsemen” (Persian places the numeral before the counted noun).
segments.s274-g3: ابا here functions as an archaic/poetic form of با (“with”) and introduces an instrumental list; because the given lemma senses do not include this use, its sense is marked separately. اسب و ساز و سلیح are a coordinated list (horse, gear/instrument, weapons), and تمام postmodifies the list as an intensifier/adjective meaning “complete/whole.”
segments.s274-g4: همه شیرمرد و همه نیک‌نام: همه (all/everyone) repeats to emphasize both coordinated nouns. شیرمرد is a compound noun “brave man/champion,” and نیک‌نام is an adjective/noun meaning “well‑reputed” or “of good name.” Word order is parallel: quantifier + noun, conjunction, quantifier + quality.
segments.s274-g5: همانگه is an archaic temporal adverb meaning “at that moment.” ز ایران indicates origin/affiliation (“from/of Iran”), and سپه پهلوان is a nominal phrase meaning “(an) army‑champion” or “a champion of the host” — noun+noun compounding is common in classical Persian.
segments.s274-g6: بخواند here is used in the sense “he called/summoned” (3rd-person singular past). آن زمان is a parenthetical temporal phrase “at that time,” and ده سوار جوان is the object: numeral + noun + adjective “ten young horsemen” (جوان qualifies سوار).
segments.s274-g7: برون is an adverb meaning “out/forth.” تاختند is a 3rd-person plural past of تاختن, here “they rushed/galloped/charged.” از میان سپاه is a prepositional phrase “out from the midst of the army” (از میان = from among), marking their point of departure.
segments.s274-g8: برفتند is 3rd-person plural past “they went/departed/advanced.” یکسر is an adverb meaning “at once/directly/altogether.” به آوردگاه is the goal phrase “to the battlefield” (به = to, آوردگاه = battlefield).
segments.s274-g73: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing the group; مردی is an indefinite noun 'men' (literary singular form used collectively). که بودند is a relative clause 'who were' with the past plural verb بودند agreeing implicitly with the men; با بخت خویش is a prepositional phrase 'with their/belonging-to-them fortune' where خوی + enclitic ش forms the reflexive possessive 'their own'.
segments.s274-g74: برآویختند is a past plural verb 'they sprang up/rose' (poetic prefixed form). از پی is an idiomatic prepositional sequence 'in pursuit of/after'; تخت خویش is 'their throne' with خوی + ش again forming the reflexive possessive. The verb in front followed by the 'از پی ...' phrase marks the action of rising up to pursue the throne.
segments.s274-g75: سران is the subject 'the chiefs/leaders'. از پی پادشاهی is an adverbial phrase 'in pursuit of kingship' (از پی = seeking/after). به جنگ is a direction/goal phrase 'to battle' (به + noun meaning 'into/to battle'), and the clause lacks an independent verb here because the action continues in the next segment.
segments.s274-g76: بدادند is the past plural of دادن with the poetic prefix ب-: 'they gave' or 'they yielded up' (i.e., sacrificed). جان is the direct object 'life'. از پی here functions idiomatically as 'for the sake of' or 'on account of', and نام و ننگ is a compound 'name (reputation/fame) and shame'—a set phrase describing what they risked or sought.
segments.s274-g77: دمان here means 'skirt/edge' (poetic usage) — 'to the edge/skirts'. آمدند is the past plural 'they came'. اندر is the locative preposition 'into/within' and آوردگاه is 'battlefield/venue of bringing', so the phrase places the arrival at the battlefield's edge.
segments.s274-g78: ابا is the verb 'to refuse / be reluctant' used here to indicate obstinacy toward the other side; یکدگر is the reciprocal 'one another'. ساخته is a perfect participle 'having made' (literally 'made'), modifying کینه 'hatred/grudge'. خواه in this position is adjectival/verb-like 'seeking/desiring' (forming the compound idea 'kineh-خواه' = 'seeking revenge'). Together the phrase says they refused to yield to one another, having made mutual hatred and seeking revenge.
segments.s274-g9: که introduces a clause ('that' / 'for'), and here begins a statement about visibility. دیدار دیده is a poetic doublet meaning 'sight of the eye' (دیدار = sight/meeting, دیده = eye). بر ایشان نبود is literal word order 'upon them was not' = 'was not upon them' → 'no eye looked upon them' / 'they could not be seen'; نبود is past negative of بودن.
segments.s274-g10: دو سالار = 'two chiefs/commanders' (numeral + noun). ز این‌گونه is a poetic adverbial phrase 'in this way / thus' (ز = 'from/of' used idiomatically), زرم آزمود = 'tested my gold' where زرم is 'my gold/golden (thing)' and آزمود is past 'tried/tested'; the phrase means the two chiefs 'tried' or 'tested' (performed an action involving) the speaker's gold/treasure.
segments.s274-g11: ابا begins the line as an archaic/poetic particle (here best rendered as a framing/expletive element) followed by هر سواری 'each horseman/rider'. ز ایران modifies سپاه (the army/host of Iran); Persian word order places modifiers after or before in poetic lines, so 'هر سواری ز ایران سپاه' reads as 'each horseman of Iran's host'.
segments.s274-g12: ز توران = 'from Turan'. یکی شد = 'one became' / 'one stood forth' (یکی used as 'one person/someone'); ورا here means 'toward him' or 'against him' (thither/to him), and رزم خواه is a compound: رزم 'battle' + خواه from خواستن 'to desire', functioning adjectivally/participial 'battle-seeking' or 'eager for combat'. The whole means 'one from Turan arose to meet him, eager for battle.'
segments.s274-g13: نهادند is the simple past 'they placed/put' (third-person plural). پس = 'then/afterwards' and گیو را is the direct object 'Giv' with the object marker را. با introduces accompaniment 'with', and گروی here is a proper name or group-name attached to Giv; although the listed senses treat گروی as a verb, in this line it functions as a name.
segments.s274-g14: که introduces a relative clause 'who'. همزور بودند = 'they were equal in strength' (همزور 'equal in strength/rivals' + بودند past plural of بودن). و پرخاشجوی = 'and quarrelsome/aggressive' (پرخاشجوی functioning as an adjective). The clause modifies گروی.
segments.s274-g15: گروی زره = 'Goru's armor' (proper name + noun; the possessive relation is implied by juxtaposition). کز میان سپاه is a relative phrase 'which/from among the host' (کز = 'that/from which', میان سپاه = 'in the midst of the army'). This segment sets up the noun being qualified in the next segment.
segments.s274-g16: سراسر = 'throughout/entirely' (degree adverb). بر او بود = 'was upon him' (بر = 'on/upon', او = 'him', بود = past of 'to be'). نفرین شاه = 'the king's curse' (نفرین 'curse' governed by شاه 'king' following it, a genitive-like construction common in poetry). Word order in Persian places the predicate 'was' before the nominative phrase in such poetic inversions: literally, 'entirely upon him was the king's curse.'
segments.s274-g41: بدان is the imperative 'know/learn' formed from the prefix ب- plus دان. تا که را... introduces an indirect interrogative ('to whom') where که is the interrogative 'who/that' and را is the object marker attached to it (archaic/orderly: 'که را' = 'whom'). گردد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive verb meaning 'turn/go to' or 'happen'; امروز کار ('the affair today') is the time + topic placed after the verb, so the clause literally: 'Know to whom the affair will turn today.'
segments.s274-g42: که begins a subordinate clause: 'who/that'. پیروز is an adjective 'victorious' describing the person. برگردد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive (poetic/future sense) 'returns/comes back'. از کارزار is a prepositional phrase 'from the battlefield' specifying the source/place of return.
segments.s274-g43: دو (implied in the line) quantifies بالا ('the high/lofty ones'); بالا here is a poetic noun/adjective 'high/lofty' (often applied to prominent things such as standards or chiefs). بد appears as a poetic modifier close to بالا (ancient/poetic use; a literal 'bad' sense is possible but here functions in context as an intensifying/qualifying element). اندر is the preposition 'in/among', and دو روی سپاه = 'the two faces/sides of the army' with روی = 'side/face' and سپاه = 'army'. Note the copula 'was/were' is omitted (a common poetic ellipsis).
segments.s274-g44: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. شایست is the adjective 'worthy/fitting' and کردن is the light verb here forming a verbal phrase 'شایست کردن' = 'to be fitting' or 'to deserve'. به هرسو نگاه is the complement: به marks direction/target ('for/to'), هرسو 'everywhere/on every side', نگاه 'a gaze/sight' — together 'worthy of being seen from every side.'
segments.s274-g45: یکی is the numeral/pronoun 'one' and introduces the first of two contrasted directions. سوی is the preposition 'toward' (direction), so 'یکی سوی ایران' = 'one toward Iran'. دگر = 'the other' contrasts with یکی, and the repeated سوی تور = 'the other toward Tur.' Word order is parallel: numeral/pronoun + سوي + proper name.
segments.s274-g46: که begins the clause 'which/that'. دیدار here functions as 'seeing/visibility' (a nominal/poetic use) and بودی is the past form 'was/were' (3rd singular past), so دیدار بودی = 'was visible/was a sight'. به لشکر ز دور is a prepositional phrase 'to/for the army from afar' (ز دور = 'from a distance'), so the clause means 'which could be seen by the army from afar.'
segments.s274-g47: به پیش 'ahead/in front' and اندرون 'within/inside' form a locative phrase ('in front, inside'). بود is the past copula 'was/were' (here indicating existence: 'there were'). هامون و دشت are the nouns in the predicate ('Hamun and the plain'). The order places the locative phrase before the verb, then the items that existed.
segments.s274-g48: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. تا here means 'until/as long as' (temporal limit). زنده 'alive' qualifies the time span. شایست is 'fitting/worthy' used predicatively and بر وی گذشت uses گذشت (past 'passed') with بر وی 'over it/on it' — together the clause says 'which, as long as he was alive, it was fitting that he cross/pass over it' (i.e. 'he ought to have crossed it while alive').
segments.s274-g33: هجیر و سپهرم is a coordinated subject (two proper names joined by و). به کردار شیر is an adverbial phrase: به + noun (کردار) used here to mean 'in the manner/conduct of' and شیر (lion) specifies the manner — literally 'in the conduct of a lion' = 'like a lion'. Word order is name(s) + adverbial phrase describing how they acted.
segments.s274-g34: بدان here is the older compound 'به + آن' meaning 'to that' or 'into that'; the segment is then رزمگاه اندر آمد دلیر = 'entered that battlefield bravely.' اندر آمد is a two-part expression where اندر (in/within) + آمد (past of آمدن, 'came') yields 'entered.' دلیر at the end is a predicative/adverbial adjective describing how they came ('bravely').
segments.s274-g35: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'). The names گودرز کشواد are coordinated with و. پیران به هم means 'the elders together' (به هم = 'together/with one another'), so the whole phrase lists the people who act when the time arrives.
segments.s274-g36: همه ساخته is a verbal phrase: همه ('all/everyone') + ساخته (past participle/verb 'made') with دل as the direct object. دل به درد و ستم ساختن is an idiomatic expression meaning 'to make the heart into/filled with pain and oppression' (دل = 'heart'; به introduces the resulting state). Word order places the object phrase before or next to the verb in poetic/older Persian.
segments.s274-g37: میان بسته is a two-word expression meaning 'bound/closed between' — here idiomatically 'a tie/hostility was formed between.' هر دو سپهبد ('each/both two spahbeds') is the subject, and به کین ('in/with hatred') states the nature of that tie. The verb sense is stative/causative 'bound themselves (in).'
segments.s274-g38: چه ... چه ... is the correlative 'whether ... or ...'. از پادشاهی = 'over/from the kingship' (آز here 'because of/regarding'), and از بهر دین = 'for the sake of religion' (بهر meaning 'for'). Together they explain the grounds of the enmity.
segments.s274-g39: بخوردند سوگند is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'they took/swore an oath' (بخوردند here used idiomatically with سوگند). یک با دگر = 'one with another' (each to the other). The verb is plural and the phrase sets up the content of the oath.
segments.s274-g40: که introduces the content of the oath ('that ...'). کس is 'no one/anyone' (context makes it negative), and برنگرداند is a third-person negative/jussive form meaning 'should not cause to return / should not be allowed to return' — here idiomatically 'shall not escape/spare.' از کینه سر is an ablative/causal phrase 'from/of the grudge's head' — poetically 'from the vengeance of the grudge,' with سر (head) used concretely for life/head (i.e., deadly vengeance).
segments.s274-g49: This is a simple past clause: subject (سپهدار گودرز, a title + name) + past verb کرد + direct object آن نشان. کرد is the past tense of کردن used here as a plain past 'made/placed'. آن نشان is a definite direct object following the verb, with آن emphasising 'that' specific mark.
segments.s274-g50: This is a relative clause introduced by که (here 'that/who'), giving the intended addressee of the sign: هر 'every/whichever' + کو (an archaic/interrogative element meaning 'who' in this context) + ز 'from' + گردان 'troop' + گردنکشان 'neck‑slayers/rebels'. Word order is typical classical Persian: quantifier + (archaic) interrogative + prepositional phrase 'ز گردان' specifying membership.
segments.s274-g51: به زیر آورد is a verbal phrase: به + زیر + آورد = 'brought under' or idiomatically 'brought down' (آورد in past). دشمنی را is the definite object (را marks it). چو دود begins a simile/clausal link: چو here functions 'as/when' and دود supplies the image 'like smoke' that modifies what follows.
segments.s274-g52: درفشی is the banner/standard (here the object being raised). ز بالا is a prepositional phrase 'from above / on high'. برآرند is a third‑person plural present/epic present 'they raise/bring up' (classical narrative uses present forms for vividness). زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying برآرند.
segments.s274-g53: Again a past clause: سپهدار (title) + پیران (here a proper name, Piran) as the subject, نشانی (object 'a sign/marker') and نهاد the past of نهادن 'placed/put'. Note that پیران in the Shahnameh is a character name rather than the common noun 'elders' — the provided lemma senses do not include the proper‑name sense.
segments.s274-g54: به بالای دیگر is a locative phrase 'on/onto the other summit/height' (به + بالای + دیگر). همین کرد یاد is an idiomatic sequence: کرد 'did' + یاد 'remembrance/memorial' — together meaning 'he did the same as a remembrance' or 'thus made a memorial'. Word order places the verb کرد before the noun یاد in the formulaic expression.
segments.s274-g55: از آن پس is a temporal adverbial 'after that/thereafter'. به هامون is a locative 'onto the plain' (به + هامون). نهادند is the past plural 'they placed/set' and سر is the direct object 'head' — here idiomatic: 'they set their heads' meaning they prepared to risk/offer their heads.
segments.s274-g56: This clause states purpose/preparation: به خون ریختن is an infinitival/gerundial phrase 'to spill blood / for blood‑shedding' (به + خون + ریختن). بسته is a past participle/adjective 'bound/tied' modifying گردان کمر: گردان 'the troop' + کمر 'waist / girdle' — together 'they girded the troop's loins' (i.e., prepared themselves by binding belts) for the coming bloodshed.
segments.s274-g57: This is a list of instruments introduced by the preposition به used as an instrumental/agentive marker (here: 'with' or 'by'). The preposition is repeated for emphasis before each major item (به تیغ و به گرز و به تیر ...) and the items are coordinated by و. کمند (snare/lasso) closes the series without a preceding به for poetic economy.
segments.s274-g58: همی is a poetic/adverbial particle indicating continued or habitual action ('continually/indeed'). آزمودند is the simple past 3rd-person plural of آزمودن ('they tried/tested'). هرگونه بند is the direct object: هرگونه 'every/any kind of' modifying بند 'bond/fetter/means of restraint'. Word order here is adverb → verb → object.
segments.s274-g59: دلیران توران is a noun phrase: دلیران (plural) 'the brave ones' qualified by توران 'of Turan' (a genitive/epithet). The coordinating و plus کنداوران adds a second plural group ('and the کنداوران'); کنداوران is an agentive plural noun formed from کنداورد / کنداوری meaning those who dig or excavate (here 'trench-diggers' or besiegers).
segments.s274-g60: ابا is the verbal/noun root meaning 'to be reluctant/refuse'. In classical/epic usage a clause like ابا + (objects) often conveys (by context and negative implication) that they did not shrink from or were not reluctant to use those things. Here گرز و تیغ و پرندآوران are the objects/activities (mace, sword, and 'پرندآوران' — literally 'those who bring fliers', i.e. archers or missile-bringers). Grammatically, the sequence expresses that these warriors were not averse to using mace, sword, and deploying archers.
segments.s274-g61: که‌گر is the fused conditional conjunction (که اگر) meaning 'if'. کوه پیش آمدی is a protasis using آمدی (past-form used for a counterfactual/hypothetical 'should come/come forward') with پیش 'in front of' or 'before them'. روز جنگ is a temporal adverbial 'on the day of battle' placed after the protasis.
segments.s274-g62: نبودی here expresses non-existence/absence ('there would not be' / 'there was no'), governing the following noun phrase. بر آن رزم کردن is a verbal nominal construction where رزم کردن (battle + light verb کردن) means 'to make war / to fight it', and بر آن marks the target 'against it'. درنگ 'delay' is the noun that completes the idea: 'there would be no delay in fighting it.'
segments.s274-g63: همه is the universal quantifier 'all' modifying دستهاشان ('their hands' — دست + possessive suffix). فروماند is a past-form verb meaning 'sank/fell/wore out' (poetically 'were brought low' or 'failed'), and پست postpositively qualifies the state as 'low' or 'humble'. The whole phrase means 'all their hands fell low' (i.e., their strength failed).
segments.s274-g64: در زور یزدان is an agentive/ablative phrase 'in the might/power of God' (در + زور + یزدان). بر ایشان ببست is a verb phrase: ببست (3rd-person past) 'he bound/closed' with بر ایشان marking the persons affected ('upon them'). Together: 'by God's might he bound them' (i.e. God overpowered/struck them down).
segments.s274-g65: به ... اندر is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'into' or 'into the snare of'; here به (preposition) + دام بلا (the noun phrase 'snare/cage of calamity') + اندر (archaic 'in/into') together form the complement. آویختند is a 3rd-person plural past tense (poetic/archaic) literally 'they hung/fastened' but here idiomatically 'they fell/were caught (in)'; the verb comes after the prepositional phrase (S‑V order typical of Persian but with archaisms).
segments.s274-g66: که introduces a following clause ('for/that/and'); بسیار here is an adverb meaning 'much/very' modifying بیداد ('injustice/oppression'). خون ریختند is a normal past plural clause 'they poured/shed blood' (خون = object before the past verb ریختند in the poetic line). ریختند is the past of ریختن 'to pour/ shed.'
segments.s274-g67: فرومانده is a participial/adjectival form 'downcast/left behind/exhausted' modifying اسبان جنگی 'war-horses' (اسبان = horses, جنگی = warlike/war-). به جای is the prepositional phrase 'in place of / in the stead of' that will attach to the following pronoun (تو). Word order: adjective + noun + qualifier + prepositional phrase, typical of classical poetic Persian.
segments.s274-g68: تو گفتی is the reporting clause 'you said' (تو = 2nd‑person pronoun, گفتی would be the 2nd‑person past of گفتن in ordinary prose; here the provided sense list does not include the verb sense so the exact sense index is not available). که introduces the reported content. با دست is an instrumental phrase 'with (your) hand' and precedes بستست پای, where بستست is a past form 'had bound/fastened' (archaic/poetic) and پای 'foot' is the object; the clause therefore reads 'that (someone) with (his) hand had bound the foot.' The verb is 3rd‑person past while the reporter is 2nd person ('you said that ...').
segments.s274-g69: بر ایشان is a prepositional topical phrase 'upon them.' همه راستی is the nominal phrase 'all (their) uprightness/righteousness' (همه = all; راستی = uprightness). شد is the past of شدن 'became,' and نگون is a predicate adjective 'overthrown/ill‑fated/turned upside down.' The clause literally: 'upon them all uprightness became overturned.'
segments.s274-g70: که again introduces a consequence/continuation. برگشت روز uses برگشت (return/reversal) with روز 'day' to mean 'the day turned back' or 'fortune reversed' (برگشت as reversal). و is the coordinating 'and.' بجوشید خون is a vivid past tense 'blood boiled/ gushed' (بجوشید from جوشیدن); note the poetic inversion: verb بجوشید appears before the noun خون for emphasis.
segments.s274-g71: چنان is an adverb 'thus/so' qualifying خواست 'willed/desired' (past tense). یزدان 'God' is the subject, with جان‌آفرین an epithet in apposition 'life‑giving' (a divine epithet). The verb + subject order (خواست یزدان) shows a poetic arrangement where the verb appears before or adjacent to its subject.
segments.s274-g72: که introduces the reported clause. گفتی here functions as a reportive verb 'it is said/they say' or 'someone said' in the poem (the provided sense list does not include the verbal sense, so its precise index is unavailable). گرفت is the 3rd‑person past 'seized/took.' آن گوان را is the definite object 'those ones' (آن + گوان used distributively/ demonstratively) with را the object marker, and زمین 'the earth' follows; the line uses poetic inversion: verb گرفت then object آن گوان را, and finally the agent/subject زمین ('the earth seized those').
segments.s274-g25: چو introduces a temporal clause here (“when”). گرگین is the proper name Gorgin (a hero) rather than the adjective “wolf‑like.” کارآزموده is a post‑nominal adjective “battle‑tested/experienced,” and سوار is the noun “rider/horseman.” Word order is typical Persian: temporal marker + name + modifiers.
segments.s274-g26: که is the relative pronoun “who/that,” introducing the clause describing the subject. با marks accompaniment “with,” اندریمان is a proper name (Andariman), کند is the present/historical‑present verb “does/makes/wages,” and کارزار is the object “battle/war.” The verb appears at the end, as usual in Persian.
segments.s274-g27: This is a nominal cluster listing fighters. ابا here functions as an archaic introductory particle meaning something like “also/then” (not the verb sense listed); بیژن and گیو are proper names (Bijan, Giv). رویین گرد is a compound epithet meaning “iron‑armored / iron‑hard” (رویین = iron‑hard; گرد participates in the compound). The phrase names the men who follow.
segments.s274-g28: به جنگ is a prepositional phrase “to/into battle.” از جهان روشنایی is a genitive phrase “light from the world.” ببرد is third‑person singular present/subjunctive (here used as historical/present narrative) of بردن “to carry/take,” placed at the end as the clause’s main verb: “he carries/takes.”
segments.s274-g29: چو + past verb (او خواست) = “when he wished/desired.” با introduces accompaniment “with.” زنگه شاوران is a noun phrase: شاوران = “advisers/consultants”; زنگه here likely names a person or a leader among them (the precise lexical sense is ambiguous in the supplied senses), so the whole phrase means “with Zangah and the advisers.”
segments.s274-g30: دگر = “another / other” (introduces an additional party). برته functions as a noun here (something like “a handful/tuft/bundle” or a small detachment — the exact archaic sense is context‑dependent). با again marks accompaniment “with.” کهرم is treated here as an epithet or group name (the supplied senses give ‘old age/ancient thing’ but in context it functions as a label), and از یاوران = “of/from the supporters/helpers.” The phrase names a second group accompanying the action.
segments.s274-g31: چو = “when.” دیگر = “other/another.” فروهل here is most likely a proper name (or archaic term) rather than the adjective sense listed; بد either qualifies that name as an epithet “bad/evil” or is part of a compound name. و links the next item, زنگله, which likewise may be a name (though it can also mean “thicket” in other contexts). The segment lists combatants who next act.
segments.s274-g32: برون = “out/forth.” تاختند is past plural “they rushed/galloped/attacked” (تاختن), placed before the source phrase; از میان گله = “out of/from among the flock/herd.” Overall: ‘they rushed out from among the herd.’
segments.s274-g17: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a relative clause. بگرفت is the classical past (3rd singular) 'seized' (ب‑ prefix + گرفت), followed by the object ریش سیاوش 'Siavash's beard'. به دست is an adverbial phrase 'with/by (the) hand' (به used adverbially + دست). Word order is poetic but follows verb → object → adverbial.
segments.s274-g18: سرش را is the object 'his head' with the possessive suffix -ش and the object marker را. برید is the past of بریدن 'cut' (3rd singular); the provided lemma senses do not list this meaning, so the correct sense is marked separately below. از marks separation/source 'from', and تن پاک پست is a poetic noun phrase 'pure (پاک) body (تن)' with پست appended as an epithet 'low/lowly'.
segments.s274-g19: دگر here functions adverbially 'then/again'. با + name (با فریبرز) = 'with Fariborz'. کاوس is the subject 'Kay Kavus'. تفت is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'scorched/attacked' (تفتیدن); the verb appears after the names in inverted poetic word order.
segments.s274-g20: چو = 'when'. کلباد ویسه are two proper names (Kalbad and Viseh) forming the subject/objects of the next verb. به آورد here functions as the verbal phrase 'brought' (آورد) with the prefixed or associated particle به; رفت is the following past 'he went/left', so the two verbs together indicate 'brought (and) left' — a compact, poetically compressed sequence.
segments.s274-g21: چو = 'when'. رهام گودرز are two personal names in coordination; با بارمان = 'with Barman' (با + name). The preposition با attaches Barman as a companion in the action that follows.
segments.s274-g22: برفتند is the past tense (3rd plural) 'they went/departed'. یک با دگر is an idiomatic phrase 'one with the other' (i.e., each with the other), and بدگمان is an adjective 'suspicious/distrustful' describing their mutual attitude; the adjective applies to the subject implicit in برفتند.
segments.s274-g23: گرازه here functions as an agent/name (the exact lexical sense is ambiguous in the glosses) and is marked below accordingly. بشد (ب‑شد) is an older/poetic past construction 'it came to be / he engaged', followed by با سیامک 'with Siyamak' and به جنگ 'into battle' (به as directional 'into', جنگ 'battle'). The verb is classical/poetic and the phrase means 'engaged in battle with'.
segments.s274-g24: چو introduces a simile 'like/as'. شیر 'lion' is the tenor; ژیان is a poetic qualifier (literally 'life' but here functioning adjectivally as 'living/ardent'). با introduces the other term of the simile: دمنده نهنگ = 'the breath‑blowing / spouting whale' where دمنده is a present participle 'breathing/spouting' modifying نهنگ 'whale'.

sentences.s274-s1: The Turks' commander set the affair in order, and from the host he chose — at that moment — ten horsemen.
sentences.s274-s2: With horses, equipment and full arms — all were champions and all well‑reputed.
sentences.s274-s3: At that instant a champion of Iran's host summoned ten young horsemen.
sentences.s274-s4: They dashed out from among the host and at once went to the battlefield.
sentences.s274-s37: There were men who, trusting in their fortune, sprang up in pursuit of their throne.
sentences.s274-s38: The chiefs, in pursuit of kingship, took to battle and gave their lives for the sake of name and shame.
sentences.s274-s39: They came to the skirts of the battlefield; unwilling to give way to one another, they had fashioned hatred and hungered for revenge.
sentences.s274-s5: For no eye could see them; two chiefs thus tested my gold.
sentences.s274-s6: For each horseman of Iran's host there stood one from Turan who met him and sought battle.
sentences.s274-s7: Then they set Giv with Goru (Grouy), who were equal in strength and quarrelsome.
sentences.s274-s8: Goru's armor — which was among the ranks of the host — the king's curse lay wholly upon him.
sentences.s274-s21: Find out (know) to whom the affair will turn today — who will return victorious from the battlefield.
sentences.s274-s22: Two lofty (standards/figures) stood among the two faces/sides of the host, which were fit to be looked at from every side.
sentences.s274-s23: One (looked) toward Iran, the other toward Tur; they could be seen by the army from afar.
sentences.s274-s24: Ahead, within were Hamun and the plain, which he ought to have crossed while still alive.
sentences.s274-s17: Hejir and Sepehram, like lions in their bearing, entered that battlefield bravely.
sentences.s274-s18: When Goudarz, Keshvad, and the elders together all made their hearts full of pain and oppression.
sentences.s274-s19: Between them the two spahbeds bound themselves in hatred, whether over the kingship or for the sake of religion.
sentences.s274-s20: They swore an oath to one another that no one should escape the vengeance of their grudge.
sentences.s274-s25: Commander Goudarz set that sign, so that whoever is from the troop of neck‑slayers…
sentences.s274-s26: He drove the enemy down; when, like smoke, a banner was quickly raised aloft.
sentences.s274-s27: Sepahdar Piran set a marker and placed it on the other summit, thus doing the same as a memorial.
sentences.s274-s28: After that, on the plain they laid down their heads (i.e., risked their lives to be slain) and the troop girded their loins for bloodshed.
sentences.s274-s29: They tried every kind of restraint with sword, mace, arrow, and snare.
sentences.s274-s30: The brave men of Turan and the trench-diggers did not shrink from the mace and sword and from sending forth their fliers (archers).
sentences.s274-s31: If a mountain should come forward on the day of battle, there would be no delay in making war upon it.
sentences.s274-s32: All their hands were brought low; by the might of God He bound them.
sentences.s274-s33: They fell into the snare of calamity, for they shed much unjust blood.
sentences.s274-s34: The exhausted war-horses, in your stead — you said that with (your) hand you had bound the foot.
sentences.s274-s35: Upon them all uprightness became overturned, so that the day was reversed and blood gushed forth.
sentences.s274-s36: Thus willed God, the life‑giver, that — it is said — the earth seized those (ones).
sentences.s274-s13: When Gorgin, the battle-tested horseman, who with Andariman wages battle,
sentences.s274-s14: Also Bijan, Giv and the iron‑armored (warrior) went to the fight, bringing light from the world,
sentences.s274-s15: When he wished, with Zangah the advisers — another (group) with the aged among the supporters,
sentences.s274-s16: When the other Foruhal Bad and Zangaleh burst out from among the flock,
sentences.s274-s9: who seized Siavash by the beard with his hand and cut off his head from his pure, lowly body.
sentences.s274-s10: Then Kay Kavus, with Fariborz, launched an attack; when Kalbad brought Viseh, he departed.
sentences.s274-s11: When Roham and Goudarz set out with Barman, they departed, each distrustful of the other.
sentences.s274-s12: Gorazeh came into battle with Siyamak like a fierce lion against a spouting whale.
